coredata.dc:title: Size-control synthesis of graphene quantum dots in SBA-15 micro-reactor and fabrication of imprinted polymer-based fluorescence sensor for bisphenol A recognition

coredata.dc:description: This study develops a fluorescent analytical strategy for the determination of bisphenol A (BPA). The fluorescence sensor utilizes SBA-15 as a micro-reactor to fabricate size-controlled graphene quantum dots (GQDs), followed by the synthesis of a GQD-supported polymer (BPA-MIP) via precipitation polymerization using BPA as the template. The fluorescence method demonstrated a direct correlation in the concentration range of 0.5 to 20.00 μmol L−1 for BPA, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.180 μmol L−1 and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.564 μmol L−1. Fluorescence decay analysis revealed a lifetime (τ) of 31.87 ns for GQDs and 31.64 ns for BPA-MIP (0.8 μmol L−1 BPA), with the reduced lifetime indicating dynamic quenching behavior, thereby confirming the sensor's capability for BPA detection. The method demonstrated excellent repeatability, with intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations of 1.18 % and 2.01 %, respectively, confirming the reliability of the developed fluorescence sensor. The analysis of selectivity revealed that the proposed fluorescence sensor is highly effective for BPA determination. The proposed fluorescence sensor probe was effectively utilized to three spiked beverage samples, resulting in satisfied recoveries between 100.40 % and 104.80 %.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study develops a fluorescent analytical strategy for the determination of bisphenol A (BPA). The fluorescence sensor utilizes SBA-15 as a micro-reactor to fabricate size-controlled graphene quantum dots (GQDs), followed by the synthesis of a GQD-supported polymer (BPA-MIP) via precipitation polymerization using BPA as the template. The fluorescence method demonstrated a direct correlation in the concentration range of 0.5 to 20.00 μmol L−1 for BPA, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.180 μmol L−1 and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.564 μmol L−1. Fluorescence decay analysis revealed a lifetime (τ) of 31.87 ns for GQDs and 31.64 ns for BPA-MIP (0.8 μmol L−1 BPA), with the reduced lifetime indicating dynamic quenching behavior, thereby confirming the sensor's capability for BPA detection. The method demonstrated excellent repeatability, with intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations of 1.18 % and 2.01 %, respectively, confirming the reliability of the developed fluorescence sensor. The analysis of selectivity revealed that the proposed fluorescence sensor is highly effective for BPA determination. The proposed fluorescence sensor probe was effectively utilized to three spiked beverage samples, resulting in satisfied recoveries between 100.40 % and 104.80 %.

Figure: Fluorescence detection of BPA via proposed sensor method.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a chemical compound employed in conjunction with other substances for the production of specific polymers and resins [1]. It is also known as an estrogen endocrine disruptor, which means that it conveniently accumulates in the body, causes cancer, and is harmful to the reproductive system [2,3]. Certain plastic products are utilized in containers for packing food and beverages. When these containers are subjected to heat or submerged in a water environment, BPA frequently leaches out, resulting in significant contamination [4,5]. In this regard, it is of the utmost importance to develop techniques for tracking and recognizing compounds including BPA in beverage matrices to preserve human health.

Currently, instrumental analysis techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [7] are predominantly utilized. These instruments are expensive, require intricate pre-treatment processes, specialized handling, and are time-intensive, rendering them inappropriate for simple and rapid analysis [8], and well-trained operators and complicated sample preparation [9] Thus, there is an urgent need for sensitive and precise measurement techniques to determine the amount of BPA in beverages samples. Fluorescence determination utilizing carbon quantum dots (QDs) offers a cost-effective, and highly sensitive method for the detection of BPA [10,11]. Hui et al. [12] developed a novel sensing strategy for bisphenol A (BPA) utilizing GQDs and peroxidase, and with the detection limit was as low as 0.4 nM. Similarly, khan et al., integrated Plastic antibodies with graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of bisphenol-A in tap water [13]. Molecular imprinting technology mimics antigen–antibody recognition to create molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) featuring specific binding sites [14]. The integration of the high selectivity of MIP with the more effective fluorescence response characteristics of quantum dots facilitates the development of high-performance MIP fluorescent sensors [15]. Furthermore, research shows that electrochemical sensors for BPA determination exhibit high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, adequate selectivity, straightforward instrumentation, ease of miniaturization, and equipment portability [16–18]. The selective enrichment of target compounds by combining QDs-MIPs has the potential to enhance the determination sensitivity of fluorescence sensing [19]. Liu et al. [20] employed a non-covalent method to create QDs-conjugated MIP for the selective detection of BPA in water, achieving a detection limit of 1.5 nmol L−1. Generally, Quantum dots (QDs) served as excellent fluorescent probes for determination of hazardous compounds in actual samples [21].

Quantum dots (QDs) are a form of semiconductor that can be combined with MIPs for sensor development. The optical properties of QDs are determined by the size, shape, and composition of the substance being used. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are typically synthesized from small organic molecules through methods such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal treatment, or solvothermal treatment [22]. GQDs, in contrast to traditional metal quantum dots, exhibit fewer toxic effects and enhanced compatibility due to the absence of metal ions. As a result, they have found extensive applications in fluorescence imaging, sensing, and optoelectronic materials research [23–25]. GQDs are typically derived through oxidative exfoliation of graphene. However, this approach yields quantum dots with a wide size distribution and low production efficiency, rendering it unsuitable for practical commercial use.

The microreactor synthesis technique is a method used to synthesize novel nanomaterials. The products produced exhibit a high degree of uniformity owing to the dimensions of the micro-reactor. SBA-15 is a widely used mesoporous silicon material characterized by its consistent pore structure and compact dimensions, which make it highly suitable for micro-reactor applications [26,27]. The utilization of the SBA-15 micro-reactor can significantly enhance the production efficiency of GQDs and facilitate the synthesis of quantum dots with consistent and uniform dimensions. GQDs exhibit superior characteristics such as chemical inertness, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and great photostability when compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [28].GQDs possess a high concentration of carboxyl [29] and hydroxyl groups around their edges, which results in their remarkable water solubility and ease of modification. GQDs have garnered increasing attention due to their exceptional physical and chemical characteristics, including a high surface area, a greater length-to-diameter ratio (the ratio of length to thickness), and improved surface grafting through the π-π conjugated network facilitated by their structural graphene layers [30]. These distinctive properties make GQDs highly promising for applications in sensors. The utilization of GQDs in fluorescence has grown due to alterations in the behavior of electron donors and acceptors caused by surface modification of the functional groups on the GQD surface. The excellent selectivity of MIPs combined with the high sensitivity of GQDs-based fluorescence sensors could reduce the limits of detection (LODs), allowing the determination of target analytes at trace levels. MIP-GQDs sensors develop a detectable optical change during the specific rebinding to the target analyte.

This study, present a preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using an SBA-15 as microreactor that containing rich graphene domains, Furfuryl Alcohol (FA) act as carbon source and an HNO3 oxidize carbon materials, introducing oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., –COOH, –OH) on GQDs. Encouraged by the fluorescence characteristics and external oxygen-containing groups of the GQDs integrated with molecular imprinted polymers (MIP) to investigated their application as fluorescent sensor probe for BPA determination in real samples. Furthermore, the resulting (BPA-MIP) in a fluorescent sensor probe that is both highly sensitive and selective. The fluorescent sensor probe was successful testing on beverage samples, exhibiting elevated recovery rates and efficient performance in environmental safety assessment.
The supplementary information contains details about the materials, instrument information, and experimental conditions for each section of the experiment (S1.1, and S1.2).
The optimization conditions (Table S1) and fluorescence analysis have been addressed in the supplementary materials, including in Section 2.1.

The efficacy of synthesized BPA-MIP was evaluated using mineral water, carbonated beverages, and orange juice purchased from the local market in Zhenjiang City, Jiangsu Province. Add 20 mL of methanol to 5 mL of the real sample that needs to be analyzed, then sonicate for 30 min. Transfer the mixture into a glass tube and subject it to centrifugation at a speed of 4000 rpm/min. Then, filter the mixture using a 0.22 μm filter to prepare it for analysis. Upon analyzing the filtered real samples under optimal conditions, no fluorescence signal was observed, which suggests that the matrix was free of BPA (analyte). Subsequently, BPA concentrations of 5.00 and 10.00 μmol L−1 were introduced into the filtered actual samples under optimal conditions of 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer at pH 7.

The adsorption study involved the addition of 0.003 g of each BPA-MIP (or BPA-NIP) to 0.002 mL of analyte solutions at varying concentrations. The mixtures were agitated in a rotary shaker for 2 h, after which a filter was employed to separate the solution from BPA-MIP, and the analysis was conducted using spectrophotometer, and samples were monitored at wavelength of 277 nm. The biding experiments were performed using 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer at pH 7.0. The binding capacity was determined as outlined in Eq. (1).
(1)
Q
e =
Co − Ce
m
× V
where (g) is the mass of BPA-MIP (or BPA-NIP), V (L) is the total volume of the solution, Ce (mg L−1) belongs to the equilibrium analyte concentration, and Q (mg g−1) is the amount adsorbed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based on SBA-15 micro-reactor
ce:section.ce:para: A similar synthesis of GQDs (C/SBA-15) has been documented in the literature [31]; however, this study incorporates a minor change in the synthesis of GQDs on the SBA-15 micro-reactor. A 12-h vigorous stirring period was used to disperse 1.0 g of SBA-15 into an ethanol solution containing 1.0 g of AlCl3. Subsequently, transfer the mixture onto a rotary evaporator and proceed to evaporate it until complete dryness is achieved at a temperature of 50 °C. Following that, the solid was introduced into a tube furnace and subjected to combustion in the presence of air at a temperature of 550 °C for 5 h, resulting in the formation of AlSBA-15. To impregnate the surface, take 0.25 g of prepared AlSBA-15, add 0.5 mL of furfuryl alcohol (FA), and then remove any leftover FA in an oven with a temperature of 80 °C. The PFA-SBA-15 solid was subsequently placed in a tube furnace. The sample was initially heated to 150 °C for a duration of 3 h, after which the temperature was increased to 300 °C at rates of 10 °C/min and 5 °C/min. The temperature was subsequently held at 300 °C for an additional four hours and polycondensation was conducted with a continuous nitrogen gas flow rate of 10 mL/min. To attain C/SBA-15, a small layer of carbon was grown on the inside of the AlSBA-15 channel. To elute GQDs, weigh 0.5 g of C/SBA-15 and add 5 mL of concentrated HNO3 to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. Following responding at 160 °C for 5 h, a yellow filtrate ought to be obtained. To remove excess acid, add 500 mL of deionized water for permeation. Then, concentrate the solution using rotary evaporation. Finally, freeze-dry the GQDs until they are uniform in size (Fig. 1 ).

The synthesis of MIP/NIP followed by reported literature with a minor modification [32]. In a 100 mL three-necked flask, introduce 20 mL of methanol and 50 mg of graphene oxide (GO) for ultrasonic dispersion for a duration of 30 min. Then added, 1.0 mmol of BPA, 4.0 mmol of MMA, 16 mmol of MAP, and 10 mL of GQDs in a methanol solution with a concentration of 50 mg L−1 for self-assembly, and stir the mixture overnight. After transferring the mixture to a water bath, add 10 mmol EGDMA and 5 mg AIBN, which were polymerized at 50 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere. The product was dried under a vacuum and washed with methanol following a 6-h polymerization process. For the Soxhlet extraction and washing process, employ a solution consisting of methanol and acetic acid in a volumetric ratio of 4:1. Subsequently, subject the extracted material to vacuum drying. The resulting product will be a surface molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor specifically designed for detecting bisphenol A (BPA), referred to as BPA-MIP (Fig. 2 ). The process used to prepare surface non-molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (BPA-NIP) corresponds to the BPA-MIP sensor, except that it does not include the inclusion of the template molecule BPA.

FTIR analysis was carried out to evaluate the functionality present in PFA-SBA-15, C/SBA-15, BPA-MIP, and GO. Fig. 3 (A) displays the spectra of PFA-SBA15, C/SBA-15, BPA-MIP, and GO. The band at 1712 cm−1 [33] in PFA-SBA-15 indicates the presence of carbonyl groups (C=O) resulting from oxidation. In contrast, the band at 1617 cm−1 represents the retention of the furan ring's CC stretching, suggesting that the furan structure remains intact throughout the polymerization process and its integration into the SBA-15 matrix. The 1076 cm−1 band in SBA-15 represents the Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching inside the SBA-15 framework [34], band at 3500 cm−1 is associated with CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of residual ethanol or organic groups.

The absence of peaks at 1712 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1 when comparing C/SBA-15 with PFA-SBA-15 signifies the efficient operation of the high-temperature carbonization process. The observed CC stretching at 1628 cm−1 and the 1130 cm−1 band may correspond to CO bond vibrations, indicating that the graphene has been oxidized or functionalized with oxygen-containing groups. This suggests the successful formation of GQDS without the use of commercial graphene. In GO, prominent characteristic peaks include OH stretching vibration at 3447 cm−1 [35], CO stretching at 1730 cm−1, CC aromatic rings at 1628 cm−1 [36], and CO at 1054 cm−1 [36]. In BPA-MIP, polymerization on the surface of GQDs results in the emergence of new peaks attributed to the polymeric matrix. These include characteristics of carbonyl CO stretching vibration from MMA and EGDMA in the range of 1730 cm−1 [37,38], as well as CH stretching vibrations from the methyl groups in between 2852 and 3010 cm−1 [39], Distinct CH bending vibrations associated with CH3 groups have been identified at around 1450 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 [37]. Furthermore, the aromatic CC stretching of L-phenylalanine is observed at band of 1600 cm−1 and presence of a peak at 1130 cm−1 indicative of the CO stretching vibration associated with ester linkages [40]. This suggests that the functional groups of GQDs, including CO, CC, and C-OH, have been successfully obscured by the newly established polymer network, thereby confirming the integration of the target molecules into the MIP matrix.

XRD investigation was conducted to examine the crystalline structure and phase composition of the synthesized AlSBA-15, C/SBA-15, BPA-MIP, and GO. Fig. S1 displays the small-angle XRD patterns of AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15. The XRD spectra of AlSBA-15 (black) confirm the structural integrity and successful incorporation of aluminum into the SBA-15 mesoporous silica framework. In the low-angle region, different diffraction peaks, notably the strong 100 reflection in addition to weaker peaks at 110 and 200, represent the highly ordered 2D hexagonal mesostructured characteristic of SBA-15 [41]. The XRD spectra of C/AlSBA-15 (red), heated at 80 °C in the presence of FA to yield C/SBA-15 (carbonized SBA-15), exhibit a significant low-angle diffraction peak near 1° (2θ), which corresponds to the (100) plane of the hexagonally ordered mesoporous structure of SBA-15 (Fig. S1). The diminished intensity of the (110) and (200) diffraction peaks in the resultant C/SBA-15 can be attributed to the carbon coating and the altered scattering behavior [42]. The diffraction peak of GO occurs at 2θ = 11.02° (Fig. 3(B): insert), corresponding to the (001) crystal plane of GO [43], signifying the successful synthesis of the self-produced GO. Following the formation of the molecular imprinting layer on its surface, the distinctive peak of GO shifted to 2 θ = 12.96° due to the functionalities, showing that the structure of GO remained unchanged (Fig. 3(B)). Furthermore, novel diffraction peaks emerge at 2 θ = 29.40° and 42.20°, attributable to the synthesis of GO surface-imprinted polymers.

To examine the surface morphology and structural features of the synthesized materials, SEM images were obtained for (a) AlSBA-15, (b) C/SBA-15, (c) GO, and (d) BPA-MIP. Fig. 4 , presents SEM and TEM images of the synthesized materials, providing a comprehensive examination of their morphology and structural characteristics. Figure (a) presents a SEM image of AlSBA-15, demonstrating a well-ordered short rod-like structure typical of SBA-15 materials, which generally display a hexagonally ordered mesoporous arrangement. The rod-like entities are densely arranged, suggesting the development of uniform mesopores with a consistent structure throughout the entirety. The morphology of AlSBA-15 indicates a strong structure that can preserve its integrity under diverse conditions, essential for determination and adsorption applications.

In contrast, image (b) illustrates the morphology of C/SBA-15 post‑carbonization, revealing a more amorphous and disordered structure. This morphological modification signifies effective carbon integration into the SBA-15 structure. This change in morphology indicates successful carbon incorporation into the SBA-15 framework. The modified structure, though somewhat irregular, could improve surface interaction properties for specific applications that require increased surface roughness. Figure (c) presents the SEM image of commercially available graphene oxide (GO), displaying a sheet-like structure with notable wrinkles and folds, typical of graphene materials, suggesting a high surface area and multilayered arrangement. The C/SBA-15 structure exhibits greater deviation and reduced layering compared to commercial GO, characterized by a more collapsed, amorphous morphology. This indicates that the synthesis process involving FA and nitric oxide led to decreased structural order and aggregation relative to the well-defined sheets of commercial GO. The synthesized GQDs, such as C/SBA-15, demonstrate increased surface roughness and heterogeneity, potentially improving their reactivity and surface interactions in targeted determination or adsorption applications.

TEM analysis was conducted to further investigate the internal structure of (e) AlSBA-15 and (f) C/SBA-15. The TEM image in Figure (e), proves the mesoporous structure of AlSBA-15, exhibiting the defining parallel channels and consistent pore distribution. The internal channel structure of AlSBA-15, characterized by well-defined pores, agree the results from the adsorption-desorption isotherms, which indicated the presence of type IV isotherms. Image (f) of C/SBA-15, however, exhibits a less organized architecture with discernible carbon layers, signifying the existence of carbon nanostructures. The chaotic, layered appear is indicative of graphene-like structures, validating the effective synthesis of GQDs. The decrease in structural order from AlSBA-15 to C/SBA-15, along with a development of layered carbon characteristics.

N₂ adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution analyses were conducted to assess the textural properties, such as pore volume, and pore size distribution of AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15 samples. Fig. 4(G) presents, the pore structure characteristics of AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15, as determined by nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis. AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15 demonstrate type IV isotherms characterized by significant hysteresis loops, which confirm the existence of mesoporous structures within both materials. The AlSBA-15 sample, indicated by black color, exhibits a greater total volume of nitrogen adsorption in comparison to the C/SBA-15 sample, represented by red color. This difference indicates that AlSBA-15 possesses a higher pore volume and potentially enhanced pore accessibility. The hysteresis loops are observable in the relative pressure range of approximately 0.4 to 0.9, signifying the capillary condensation phenomena characteristic of mesoporous. The inset in the figure presents the pore size distribution curves for AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15, indicating that the pore diameters are primarily centered at 6.22 nm for AlSBA-15 and 5.10 nm for C/SBA-15. This suggests that AlSBA-15 possesses a marginally larger average pore diameter than C/SBA-15. The distinct peaks identified in the pore size distribution further validate the uniformity of the mesoporous structure in both materials. The BET specific surface area of AlSBA-15 and C/SBA-15 were 478.7823 m2/g and 354.9132 m2/g, respectively. The pore diameter volume was 0.7911 cm3/g for AlSBA-15 and 0.5619 cm3/g for C/SBA-15. This evidence indicates that FA has been successfully integrated into the internal structure of AlSBA-15, resulting in the formation of a carbon layer and the synthesis of GQDS. The morphological study of GQDs via TEM reveals exceptional dispersion of nanostructures (Fig. 4(H)) devoid of aggregates, with a quasi-spherical morphology averaging 2.12 ± 1.09 nm; this outcome indicates a restricted distribution of the nanoparticles. The red circle indicates the dispersion of GQDs. The effective synthesis and resultant quasi-spherical morphology correspond well with previously published literature [44,45].

To investigate the optical properties of the synthesized GQDs, their excitation and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the range of 200 to 700 nm. Fig. 5 illustrates, the excited and fluorescence emission spectra of BPA-MIP is due to the presence of embedded GQDs, featuring a significant absorption peak at excitation of 340 nm, attributable to the π → π* transition of the aromatic CC sp2 domains while emission spectra is 512 nm. Additionally, a shoulder peak at 300 nm is seen, attributed to the n → π* transition of the CO bond and the transition of the conjugate structure [46,47].

Under UV lamp with wavelength of 365 nm, this results strong blue fluorescence (inset Figure a-b). Furthermore, the investigation of the quenching behavior of the synthesized BPA-MIP in the target analyte (BPA) was conducting, utilizing an excitation wavelength range of 340 nm to an emission wavelength of 512 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic insights into fluorescence diminution in BPA-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the mechanism driving the fluorescence reduction seen in BPA-MIP, mechanistic insights were investigated and examined. The various oxygen-containing groups present on surface of GQDs, as confirmed by FTIR analyses, may also serve as the primary contributing factor for this phenomenon. When a BPA molecule interacts with or is trapped in the cavity of BPA-MIP, the oxygen-containing groups facilitate an electron transfer mechanism between the BPA-MIP and BPA, resulting in the disruption of the fluorescence of the BPA-MIP. The disruption of the fluorescence processes may be accomplished due to the electron acceptor properties of BPA, which are facilitated by the oxygen-containing functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl) present on BPA-MIP. The electron transfer process disturbs the BPA-MIP fluorophore in excited state, which determines the generation of fluorescence. Consequently, the energized electrons in the BPA-MIP revert to the ground state via non-radiative mechanisms (e.g., energy transfer or electron transfer) instead of by radiative decay, which generates fluorescence [48,49]. The overall fluorescence intensity diminishes when the electrons transition to the ground state without producing photons, a process known as non-radiative decay. Quenching results in a decrease in fluorescence Fig. 6 (A–B) demonstrate two fluorescence decay components of GQDs: τ = 31.87 ns, and for BPA-MIP at a BPA concentration of 0.8 μmol L−1, τ = 31.64 ns. This similar behavior of life time decay agrees with dynamic quenching [50]. The fluorescence quenching process in BPA-MIP was determined to be dynamic, and the presence of electron transfer mechanism between BPA-MIP and BPA has been validated.

The fluorescence response profile of the BPA-MIP sensor was evaluated in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer at pH 7.0, using BPA concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 20.00 μmol L−1, with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 365 nm and 512 nm, respectively. Fig. 6(C) (insert) depicts the analytical curve (Fo/F vs Concentration μmol L−1) and fluorescence single decrease (FL vs Wavelength nm) of BPA-MIP. After 30 min of incubation with BPA-MIP, the model analyte solution's fluorescence single intensity gradually decreases as BPA concentrations increase. A linear calibration curve was developed within a concentration range of 0.5 to 20 μmol L−1, yielding a regression coefficient of 0.9951 (Fig. 6(C)). The detection limit was determined: 0.180 μmol L−1 (
3.3 × σ
Slope
) and limit of quantification was calculated: 0.564 μmol L−1 (
10 × σ
Slope
) . The measured KSV for BPA-MIP is 79,600 L mol−1, as illustrated in Fig. 6(C), with an imprinting factor of 9.78. The values were deemed adequate, since the sensor demonstrated great sensitivity in the fluorescence detection of the analyte at low concentrations. The quenching profile of BPA-NIP is shown in (Fig. S2, Section S 3.2). The linear calibration curve was constructed from 0.5 to 20 μmol L−1, with values of KSV for NIP is 8140 Lmol−1 and a linear correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9632.

A spectrophotometric approach for detecting BPA was employed in addition to fluorescence measurements to validate the analytical method. A calibration curve Fig. S3 (Section 3.2) was constructed for the concentration range of 10 to 1000 μmol L−1, demonstrating a robust linear correlation with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.999, a slope of 0.00125, and an intercept of 0.0089. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were established at 6.6 μmol L−1 (
3.3 × σ
Slope
) and 20 μmol L−1 (
10 × σ
Slope
) , respectively. Comparative validation results indicate that the fluorescence sensor probe exhibits markedly enhanced sensitivity, with a lower detection limit and superior quantification capacity relative to the spectrophotometric approach, underscoring its advantage for trace-level BPA determination.

In order to confirm these results, Fig. S7 (Section 3.5) shows the results of an evaluation of the adsorption capacity of BPA binding on both BPA-MIP and BPA-NIP at 25 °C using two concentration levels (2.28 and 9.13 mg L−1). The sorption capacity of BPA-MIP for BPA exceeded (1.32 and 5.10 mg g−1) that of BPA-NIP, demonstrating the presence of imprinted cavities and binding sites formed during cross-linking reactions in BPA-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of incubation time on the efficacy of the sensor probe
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of time was conducted using binding times ranging from 0 to 60 min. The results of the proposed BPA-MIP are shown in Fig. S4 (Section S 3.3). The experiments utilized 10 mL of a 5 μmol L−1 BPA solution combined with a 50 μmol L−1 BPA-MIP dispersion. The relative intensity (F0/F) exhibits significant changes upon the initial introduction of BPA, primarily due to the specific recognition of the target BPA by the surface-active sites (BPA-MIP). After 45 min, the active site becomes progressively saturated with the BPA model analyte, reaching a state of constant characteristic fluorescence. The F0/F value is currently stable. Consequently, 45 min is identified as the optimal determination duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability and influence of pH on the performance of the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The dependability and environmental stability of the fluorescence probe were extensively evaluated using repeatability evaluation and the impact of pH on its performance. Fig. S5 (Section 3.4) depicts an analysis of the pH range (2–9) of BPA-MIP to determine BPA. The optimal interaction between model analyte BPA and the cavities of the polymer matrix is observed at a pH of 7.0. Phenols and carboxylic acids usually undergo deprotonation in neutral to alkaline environments [51], increasing the accessibility of negatively charged oxygen-containing groups, as result sensor probe (BPA-MIP) demonstrates high determination efficiency for the target analyte BPA at pH 7.00, where deprotonation enhances charge transfer interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking [52] on the surface of BPA-MIP. The identification of BPA using the BPA-MIP-based fluorescence sensor probe at pH 7.00 is documented by prior research indicating that BPA establishes robust hydrogen bonds and π–π interactions with graphene-based materials within the pH range of 3.00 to 7.00 [53], via pH 7 getting within the optimal range for binding interactions.

Surface deprotonation: At pH 7, numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs, including carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and epoxide groups, are either partially or completely deprotonated. The deprotonation of these groups generates negatively charged sites on the surface of GQDs. This alteration in surface charge amplifies interactions with the electron-dense BPA molecules. The deprotonated oxygen-containing groups on GQDs enhance their capacity to function as electron acceptors. BPA, characterized by its aromatic structure and electron-donating hydroxyl groups, can more efficiently transfer electrons to the deprotonated GQDs at pH 7. The electron transfer process is a primary contributor to fluorescence dynamic quenching, as the excited fluorophore in GQDs are diminished by this interaction.

Charge transfer mechanism: The deprotonation of GQDs strengthens the charge-transfer interaction with BPA, resulting in considerable fluorescence quenching. Upon binding of BPA to the MIP sites next to GQDs, electrons from BPA's aromatic rings are transported to the conduction band of GQDs, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity and enhanced quenching efficiency.

Hydrogen bonding: In addition to facilitating electron transport, the deprotonated groups on GQDs enhance hydrogen bonding interactions with BPA. The deprotonated carboxyl groups on the GQDs and the hydroxyl groups of BPA form hydrogen bonds, bringing BPA closer to the GQD surface and intensifying the quenching effect.

π-π stacking interaction: Additionally, the aromatic rings of BPA and the graphene structure of GQDs can improve their π-π stacking interactions through deprotonation of the oxygen groups. The BPA molecules are brought even closer to the GQD surface by this stacking interaction, which increases the efficiency of electron transfer and strengthens the quenching effect.

The BPA-MIP sensor probe's intra- and inter-day repeatability was evaluated through three measurements with 0.8 μmol L−1 BPA (Fig. S6, Section S3.4). The suggested analytical method demonstrated exceptional precision and stability in the quenching measurement, as evidenced by the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the reduced fluorescence being <5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation and validation of the selectivity of the sensor probe
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor probe for evaluation was analyzed in a solution containing specific compounds, specifically 4,4′-Biphenol and tetra-bromobisphenol A. The structural details of these compounds are presented in Table S2 (Section 3.6), with a molar ratio of 1:1 and a concentration of 5.0 mg L−1 of BPA, the sensor selectivity was investigated using 5.0 mg of BPA-MIP and BPA-NIP. The selectivity evaluation and validation of the sensor probe materials are illustrated in Fig. S8(A–B). The validation of sensor selectivity was performed at a concentration of 80 μmol L−1 under optimal experimental conditions to ensure consistency and reliability. The adsorption experiments validated the sensor's preferential binding behavior. As shown in Fig. S8(A), indicates that BPA-MIP exhibited greater selectivity for BPA compared to other competitive compounds. However, BPA-NIP exhibited reduced adsorption selectivity for the BPA compound. The surface of BPA-MIP exhibits more cavities that are accessible to the target compound, whereas BPA-NIP lacks surface cavities for capturing the target compound.

The fluorescence-based method confirms the enhanced selectivity of the BPA-MIP sensor, demonstrating its ability to differentiate BPA from structurally similar analogues with high specificity, following the adsorption-based validation. The selectivity results demonstrate that BPA-MIP (sensor probe) shows a superior quenching response to BPA, while exhibiting a reduced response to Bip and TBBPA (Fig. S8(B)). This illustrates the high selectivity of the proposed BPA-MIP sensor. The results correspond with specific complementary cavities in the polymer matrix, enhancing its selectivity for the target analyte. The validation objective effectively demonstrated the potential selectivity of the proposed method (sensor probe) towards BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation of the proposed sensor probe in actual beverage samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the performance of the current sensor probe for practical applications, the sensor was utilized to detect BPA in actual samples. The efficacy of the proposed sensor has been evaluated by introducing three actual beverage samples into two different concentrations of BPA. The results obtained through this investigation are presented in Table 1 .

The proposed sensor demonstrated recovery percentages between 100.40 % and 104.8 % in the evaluation of beverage samples containing bisphenol A. Spiking the analyte with real samples revealed no significant interference in the determination procedure. The recoveries obtained from validation method were ranged from 98.02 % to 104.37 % (Table 1). Results indicate that the proposed fluorescence sensor effectively identifies BPA in liquid matrices with reliability. The comparison method's results of the BPA-MIP's values, suggesting that the sensor probe created in this work might be used as an analytical tool for determining BPA in actual samples.

A statistical analysis utilizing statistix software was performed on the determination and recovery (%) values derived from the fluorescence sensor probe and UV-spectrophotometer methods. The F-test was used to assess the equality of variances between the methods, while the t-test was employed to compare the mean values of each method to evaluate significant differences in analytical performance. A statistical analysis was conducted to compare the performance of the analytical methods regarding determination and recovery, with the corresponding values presented in Table 1.

The markedly elevated F-value in UV-spectrophotometer determination (F = 2031.49 compared to F = 678.77 for fluorescence) suggests that the UV–visible spectrophotometer method possesses superior statistical power in differentiating between groups (e.g., analyte concentrations), potentially attributable to reduce within-group variability or enhanced sensitivity to systematic differences. Both methods demonstrate statistically significant determination results (P < 0.001); however, the substantially larger F-value of the UV–visible spectrophotometer method indicates enhanced precision or a stronger signal-to-noise ratio in detecting variations. The fluorescence probe exhibits high accuracy in recovery, evidenced by a negligible F-value (F = 0.01, P = 0.911), which signifies minimal variance and no significant deviation from optimal recovery. The UV–visible spectrophotometer method presents a borderline non-significant F-value (F = 5.07, P = 0.074), indicating moderate variability or possible minor systematic errors in recovery, although the results are not statistically conclusive. Consequently, although UV–visible spectrophotometer demonstrates superior determination sensitivity, the fluorescence probe provides more dependable recovery, underscoring a trade-off between precision in determination and accuracy in analyte quantification. No significant difference was seen between the added BPA and the recovery outcomes (%) of the fluorescence sensor probe (P > 0.05). In summary, the determination and recovery values derived from our proposed fluorescence analytical approach align well with those documented in the literature [54], suggesting that the method exhibits reliable accuracy and robust performance for the quantification of bisphenol A (BPA).

Additionally, a classical statistical method was employed by comparing the calculated F-values to their corresponding critical values (F₍critical₎), utilizing the relevant degrees of freedom and a significance level of α = 0.05. The determination data indicate that both the sensor probe (F = 678.77; P < 0.001) and the UV–visible spectrophotometer (F = 2031.49; P < 0.001) produced F-values significantly above the critical threshold F₍critical₎ ≈ 6.314 (df = 1), thereby confirming statistically significant differences and supporting the rejection of the null hypothesis. Therefore, the classical statistical comparison, utilizing the calculated F-values for the sensor probe (F = 678.77) and UV–visible spectrophotometer (F = 2031.49), significantly surpasses the theoretical critical value (F₍critical₎ ≈ 6.314, α = 0.05, df = 1). This confirms that both methods exhibit statistically significant differences in determination, aligning with the p-value results (P < 0.001) in above Table 1. In contrast, the recovery (%) analysis indicates no significant difference between the methods. The F-value for the sensor probe was calculated to be 0.01 (P = 0.911), while for the UV–visible spectrophotometer it was 5.07 (P = 0.074). Both values are either below or only slightly above the critical F-value of approximately ≈ 4.96 (df = 1, at α = 0.05). The calculated F-value and p-value exhibit a marginal increase beyond their respective thresholds, with F = 5.07 exceeding the Fcritical value (Fcritical ≈ 4.96) and the p-value = 0.074 (Table 1) slightly surpassing the conventional α = 0.05 level. The result is statistically significant according to the classical F-test approach, as F exceeds Fcritical (5.07 > 4.96), despite the p-value suggesting a borderline level of significance. Similarly, the classical evaluation using Fcritical values supports the results obtained from the calculated F-statistics and corresponding p-values, indicating consistency and reinforcing the reliability of the statistical conclusions. This analysis, using the classical comparison of test statistics to critical values, confirms that both methods provide consistent recovery results, thereby reinforcing the reliability of the sensor probe compared to the UV–visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing of the proposed BPA-MIP sensor with reported literature
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of the suggested fluorescence probe was evaluated by various sensors documented in the literature. The comparison results may be found in Table 2 . The comparison analysis, which evaluated the limit of detection, operational costs, and selectivity, demonstrated that the sensor suggested in this work exhibited greater efficiency compared to previously published electrochemical sensors in the literature. In contrast to our proposed sensor, the sensors referred to in some research published in the literature require highly time-consuming and relatively more expensive synthesis techniques. For instance, these techniques involve the use of functional groups to modify the electrodes or the incorporation of two or three modifying materials. Consequently, these methods are unappealing and often difficult to implement. Additionally, fluorescence determination is typically non-destructive, saving the sample for additional examination or testing, in contrast to electrochemical techniques that could change or devour the sample during measurement. The proposed method utilizing quantum dots offers an expanded dynamic range in fluorescence-based sensors, enabling precise determination across a greater concentration spectrum than is often possible with spectrophotometric or colorimetric techniques. The sensing material (BPA-MIP) suggested in this work is simple and user-friendly. The utilized approach is significantly less time-consuming and demonstrates greater efficiency compared to previously published methods in the literature.

In conclusion, we developed a fluorescence sensor probe utilizing the GQDs-MIP composite electrode for the determination of BPA in real samples. Various characterization techniques were used such as FTIR, XRD, SEM and BET to investigate BPA-MIP, revealing information about their functional groups, shape, crystallinity, and surface area and pore diameter. The physicochemical parameters, including adsorption capacity at two concentration levels of 2.28 and 9.13 mg L−1, and adsorption capacities of 1.32 and 5.10 mg g−1, respectively, were compared with the corresponding fluorescence analyses to validate the developed method. Compared to the spectrophotometric method, which exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.63 μmol L−1 and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 20.08 μmol L−1, the fluorescence method demonstrated significantly higher sensitivity, with an LOD of 0.180 μmol L−1 and LOQ of 0.564 μmol L−1. The fluorescence lifetime of GQDs (τ = 31.87 ns) decreased to τ = 31.64 ns upon interaction with BPA-MIP at 0.8 μmol L−1, indicating dynamic quenching behavior and demonstrating the method's applicability for BPA determination. The repeatability, encompassing intra- and inter-day measurements (1.18 % and 2.01 %, respectively), demonstrated low relative error, thus confirming the effective functionality of the proposed sensor. The determination of BPA in beverage samples was supported by dynamic quenching, attributed to the movement of electrons facilitated by oxygen-containing functional groups. The sensor probe was assessed through a spectrophotometric method on three spiked beverage samples, resulting in recovery values of 100.40 % to 104.80 %. Validation of the developed sensor materials indicated recoveries from 98.02 % to 104.37 %. This finding indicates the material's excellent suitability for determine BPA in actual sample matrices and production of GQDs is simple, reliable, and eco-friendly.
